segments.title: A practical guide to medieval life
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke introduces Tony Mount's approachable guide to medieval English society, from housing and food to law, family life, and warfare.
segments.body: The book is organized around the practical questions a person would face in medieval England rather than around a conventional political narrative. Its chapters move through class and housing, belief, clothing, food, health, work, leisure, family, warfare, and law. Questions about women's work, children's education, policing, courts, military service, veterans, and survival turn those broad themes into a guide to everyday choices and constraints. The warfare chapter is therefore presented alongside the social institutions that supported or constrained it, rather than as an isolated account of fighting. This opening inventory lets viewers judge the book's breadth before Dr. Clarke reads from its chapter on pets.

evidence.note: The review lists the book's practical chapters, including its section on warfare.

segments.title: Animals as household workers and companions
segments.summary: The pets chapter shows how livestock, horses, dogs, cats, and companion animals were woven into medieval household work and daily comfort.
segments.body: The review begins its chosen chapter by placing animals inside the medieval household economy. A poorer family might share its home with a cow, goat, or chickens, gaining warmth in winter as well as keeping the animals safe at night. Horses and oxen supplied traction, dogs guarded property and herded sheep, and cats protected stored grain from vermin. Animals also supplied food, clothing, transport, and visible markers of rank, such as a knight's warhorse. Alongside those working roles, the book treats companionship as a real reason for keeping animals, including less familiar pets such as squirrels, birds, spiders, monkeys, and parrots.

evidence.note: The passage contrasts livestock and working animals with companion animals.

segments.title: Cats between pest control and suspicion
segments.summary: Cats appear as useful mousers and affordable fur, but also as animals burdened by moral suspicion and religious imagery.
segments.body: This part of the review draws out the contradictory place of cats in medieval life. They controlled rats and mice, while sumptuary laws allowed lower-status people to use cat fur for warmth. Yet Edward, Duke of York's hunting treatise could cast the cat as devilish because it tormented prey before killing it. The examples also show practical acceptance: Exeter Cathedral paid cats a small weekly allowance and provided an opening for them to pursue vermin. A guide for enclosed women allowed cats as companions but warned against loving them more than God, while records of purchases and a court dispute show that people also valued cats as possessions. A manuscript illumination adds visual evidence by depicting cats hunting rodents and birds, roaming at night, and sleeping beside the fire.

evidence.note: The review discusses cats' vermin-control role, fur, religious associations, and documentary examples.

segments.title: Dogs, work, loyalty, and medieval medicine
segments.summary: A discussion of dogs links practical tracking and guarding duties with stories of loyalty and striking medieval medical beliefs.
segments.body: Dogs are presented first as working animals that could track forest game, guard sheep, and defend an owner's property. That practical usefulness helps explain why the medieval bestiary discussed in the review devotes far more space to dogs than to cats. The review then contrasts classical stories of dogs remaining beside dead or dying masters with the politically awkward tale of Richard II's dog Math, which transferred its allegiance to Henry IV after the king's deposition. Henry interpreted the change as a favorable sign for his reign and for England's loyalty to the new regime, although Dr. Clarke treats that omen skeptically. The passage also preserves the book's reminder that medieval medical ideas could be harsh and alien to modern readers, including remedies that credited dogs' tongues or boiled puppies with healing properties.

evidence.note: The review covers dogs' working duties, classical loyalty tales, Richard II's dog, and medical remedies.

segments.title: Lap dogs as status and memorial companions
segments.summary: The review uses lap dogs in tombs and memorial brasses to show how pets could signal status, comfort, and enduring affection.
segments.body: Small dogs served as companions and visible status symbols for elite women, as well as supplying warmth in cold, draughty buildings. Dr. Clarke describes them as living hot-water bottles for their owners' hands or feet, linking affection and display to a practical domestic role. The book's memorial examples turn those private relationships into public evidence: dogs appear at the feet of women in tomb sculpture and on brasses in Sussex and Derbyshire. Clerics, dukes, and knights could also be commemorated with favored dogs. Because similar monuments appear in France and Flanders, the examples point beyond an English custom to a wider medieval aristocratic culture of status, companionship, and remembrance.

evidence.note: The passage describes lap dogs, tombs and brasses, and similar memorial customs beyond England.

segments.title: Why the book earns a recommendation
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke recommends Tony Mount's lively medieval-history guide while noting one minor error in its copyright statement.
segments.body: The closing assessment is emphatic: the book is enjoyable, worthwhile, and strong enough to make the reviewer seek out more of Tony Mount's work. Dr. Clarke briefly outlines Mount's background in historical nonfiction, teaching, medieval medicine, costume interpretation, online courses, and medieval mystery novels. Those credentials help explain the book's combination of practical detail and an accessible, lively format. The only flaw he identifies is a small gender error in the copyright declaration, not a problem with the historical material discussed in the review. He closes by saying that each book deserves its own time rather than squeezing a second review into the episode.

evidence.note: The review evaluates the book, summarizes the author's background, and identifies the minor copyright-statement error.
